Lower Extremity Amputation Rehabilitation

A focused PM&R study guide for transtibial, transfemoral, and other lower-extremity amputation rehabilitation. It integrates residual-limb care, contracture prevention, pain, prosthetic prescription, gait analysis, energy cost, and practical rehabilitation programming.

Core principlesAssessmentProstheticsGait abnormalitiesRehabilitationAnalytical quiz
12%Best-case nonvascular transtibial energy cost estimate versus able-bodied walking.
36%Typical vascular transtibial estimate from the original study notes.
Clinical focusSkin safety, contralateral-foot protection, pain control, alignment, balance, and participation.

1. Core principles and rehabilitation goals

Think function, safety, and participation.

  • Preserve useful limb length only when it supports wound healing, soft-tissue coverage, prosthetic control, and component alignment.
  • Begin rehabilitation before planned amputation or immediately after traumatic limb loss.
  • Use an interdisciplinary pathway: surgery, PM&R, nursing, physiotherapy, OT, prosthetics/orthotics, psychology, social work, vascular and diabetes care as relevant.
  • Set individual goals for wound healing, pain control, contracture prevention, transfers, mobility, ADL, participation, and protection of the contralateral limb.
Exam line
“The purpose of prosthetic rehabilitation is to restore the highest feasible level of safe, energy-efficient, and independent mobility while preventing secondary complications and improving participation.”

2. Amputation levels and surgical considerations

Level affects energy demand, prosthetic control, and mobility potential.

LevelKey rehabilitation implication
TranstibialPreserves the knee, usually supports better gait efficiency and prosthetic control than transfemoral amputation.
TransfemoralLoss of knee requires a prosthetic knee system and raises energy cost and gait complexity.
Knee disarticulationLong lever arm and end-bearing potential, but component and cosmesis constraints may occur.
Syme / ankle disarticulationEnd-bearing potential with bulbous distal limb and component-clearance considerations.
Soft-tissue closure
Myodesis generally provides more stable muscle fixation and may support distal soft-tissue control; myoplasty or myofascial closure may be appropriate depending on soft tissue and surgical context.

3. Rehabilitation phases

Each phase builds the next.

PhasePrimary prioritiesKey interventions
Pre-prosthetic managementPrepare the patient and residual limb for prosthetic use.Education, counseling, edema control, limb shaping, ROM, strengthening, balance, transfers, skin protection.
Immediate postoperative careWound healing and prevention of early complications.Wound care, pain control, edema management, positioning, early mobility, contracture prevention.
Prosthetic fitting and trainingSafe and functional prosthetic use.Socket fit review, don/doff, skin checks, standing balance, gait, stairs, community mobility.
Long-term follow-upMaintain function and respond to change.Component review, limb-volume change, pain and skin surveillance, fall prevention, conditioning, participation goals.

4. Residual-limb assessment and pre-prosthetic management

Never assess the prosthesis without assessing the person and the residual limb.

Core assessment domains

  • Residual limb: wound, scar, edema, shape, volume, bony prominence, tenderness, neuroma, ROM, strength, skin integrity.
  • Contralateral limb: sensation, pulses, callus, ulcer, deformity, footwear, strength and ROM.
  • Prosthesis: socket fit, suspension, alignment, sock ply, wear schedule, skin response, components and don/doff skill.
  • Function: transfers, sit-to-stand, standing tolerance, gait, stairs, turning, ADL, community mobility.
  • Person/environment: cognition, vision, cardiopulmonary reserve, caregiver support, home, work and goals.
Diabetes or dysvascular caution
Document residual-limb and contralateral-foot skin status before and after higher-load therapy or prosthetic progression. Persistent erythema, blistering, skin breakdown, new ulceration or disproportionate pain requires reassessment.
Edema and limb shaping
Options include soft dressings, removable rigid dressings, shrinker socks, elastic compression, and temporary preparatory systems when wound and medical status allow.

5. Contracture prevention and positioning

Positioning is treatment, not an afterthought.

ContractureWhy it developsEffect on standing and gaitPrevention
Hip flexionProlonged sitting or reclined posture, weak hip extensors, pain-related avoidance of extension.Forward trunk/pelvic compensation, poor upright alignment, shorter contralateral step length.Avoid prolonged hip-flexed positioning; regular prone lying if appropriate; hip-extension ROM; gluteal strengthening.
Knee flexion in transtibial amputationPillow under knee, wheelchair sitting, wound or neuroma pain, hamstring shortening, quadriceps weakness.Crouched standing, higher quadriceps demand, lower passive stability, reduced prosthetic-side loading, higher energy cost.No pillow under knee; maintain knee extension in sitting and lying; knee-extension ROM; quadriceps strengthening.
Standing biomechanics
With a knee flexion contracture, the ground-reaction force tends to remain posterior to the knee, producing an external knee-flexion moment. The quadriceps must work continuously to prevent collapse, which increases fatigue and reduces standing tolerance.

6. Pain after amputation

Identify the pain generator before selecting treatment.

ConditionTypical descriptionAssessment focusManagement principles
Residual-limb painPain localized to the remaining limb.Skin, scar, socket pressure, infection, ischemia, bony prominence, heterotopic ossification, neuroma.Treat the cause; socket review; wound and skin care; graded loading; multimodal analgesia.
Phantom sensationNon-painful perception from the missing body part.Exteroceptive, kinetic, kinesthetic, telescoping phenomena.Education and reassurance unless distressing or functionally limiting.
Phantom limb painPain perceived in the absent limb, often electrical, shooting, squeezing, cramping, or burning.Quality, intensity, triggers, sleep, mood, residual-limb pathology, central sensitization.Multimodal programme: education, graded motor imagery or mirror therapy where suitable, medication review, sleep and mental-health support, prosthetic optimisation.
NeuromaFocal neuropathic pain from a transected nerve end.Localized tender point, positive Tinel sign, shooting/electric pain, socket-pressure provocation, ultrasound if needed.Unload painful pressure, socket modification, desensitize around the site, neuropathic pain management, specialist referral when persistent and disabling.
Neuroma mechanism
A traumatic neuroma develops when the proximal end of a transected peripheral nerve regenerates without an appropriate distal target, creating a disorganized proliferation of axons, Schwann cells, and scar tissue. Pain is related to ectopic firing and mechanical hypersensitivity.
Definitive treatment
Persistent, focal, function-limiting neuroma pain may require surgical management. Contemporary approaches such as targeted muscle reinnervation (TMR) or regenerative peripheral nerve interface (RPNI) aim to provide a biologic target for regenerating axons. Specialist selection is essential.

7. Prosthetic prescription, components, and K levels

Prescribe a system, not just a limb.

  • Consider limb level, residual-limb shape and length, skin and soft tissue, ROM, strength, balance, cognition, comorbidities, functional classification, environment, work, participation goals, funding and maintenance.
  • For diabetic or dysvascular users, prioritize skin protection, easy donning, pressure distribution, reliable suspension, contralateral-foot protection, and frequent review for volume change.
  • Socket and component selection must be iterative: pain, skin response, gait deviation, falls, function, and patient preference all matter.

PTB: purposeful loading of pressure-tolerant regions such as patellar tendon, with relief over sensitive bone, tibial crest, fibular head, distal tibia, scar, and neuroma areas.

TSB: broader load distribution, often using a gel liner. In practice, many sockets blend PTB and total-contact principles.

PTB-SC/SP: higher trim lines can add mediolateral stabilization and suspension, useful for a short limb or knee instability.

K levels describe expected functional mobility and environmental demand. Use them alongside real-world performance, goals, cognition, cardiopulmonary reserve, and safety rather than as a stand-alone prescription tool.

8. Gait abnormalities in lower-extremity amputation

Observe the deviation, identify the likely cause, then adjust alignment, fit, components, or rehabilitation.

Amputee gait deviations are rarely explained by one factor alone. Review socket fit and suspension, alignment, prosthetic length, component stiffness, residual-limb ROM and strength, pain, balance, and the patient’s walking strategy.

Clinical reading sequence

  1. Initial contact and loading response: foot position, heel lever, knee stability, trunk response.
  2. Midstance: socket thrust, pelvic position, trunk lean, prosthetic-side confidence.
  3. Terminal stance and pre-swing: heel rise, toe lever, limb progression, step length.
  4. Swing: toe clearance, prosthetic length, suspension, hip hiking/circumduction, cadence symmetry.
Exam line
“I will first identify the phase and visible deviation, then distinguish a prosthetic cause, such as alignment or socket fit, from a patient factor, such as weakness, contracture, pain, or balance impairment.”

High-yield transtibial gait deviations

Observed deviationCommon prosthetic or patient contributorsFirst clinical response
Abrupt heel contact or rapid knee flexion at loading responseExcessive heel lever or heel stiffness; unstable loading strategy.Review foot alignment and heel stiffness; retrain controlled loading.
Prolonged heel contact or knee remains flexedInadequate heel lever, worn heel, inadequate socket flexion, or fear of loading.Check heel condition, alignment, socket flexion and quadriceps control.
Jerky knee motionLoose socket, poor alignment, inadequate suspension, or inconsistent loading.Inspect socket fit and suspension first; then reassess alignment and gait training needs.
Medial or lateral socket thrust in midstanceFoot too far inset/outset, loose socket, frontal-plane alignment error.Review socket fit and coronal-plane alignment; consider socket revision if volume/fit is the issue.
Early heel rise or abrupt “drop-off”Prosthesis too short or too long, inadequate toe lever, or excessive prosthetic-side push-off demand.Check prosthetic length and forefoot/toe lever alignment.
Heel-off too early or excessively delayedExcessive toe lever or socket extension; alternatively inadequate toe lever or socket flexion.Review foot position, toe lever, and socket sagittal alignment.
Prosthetic foot drag in swingProsthesis too long, poor suspension, hip flexion contracture, reduced hip flexor strength, poor confidence.Check length and suspension, assess hip ROM/strength, then train swing clearance.
Uneven step lengthAsymmetrical weight acceptance, pain, contracture, length discrepancy, inadequate gait confidence.Identify whether the short step is on the sound or prosthetic side; then check loading, length, ROM and pain.
Safety point
Do not treat a visible gait deviation with alignment changes alone. Reassess skin response, residual-limb pain, socket tolerance, falls, contracture, hip strength, cardiopulmonary capacity, and the patient’s functional goals after every modification.

9. Functional classification and gait energy cost

Energy cost is individual, not a fixed number.

Level / situationEnergy cost finding in original notesClinical interpretation
Nonvascular transtibial with prosthesisAbout +12% vs able-bodied walking in a best-case group.Often healthier, traumatic/nonvascular users with better reserve.
Vascular transtibial with prosthesisAbout +36% vs able-bodied walking.Diabetes, vascular disease, age, deconditioning, pain, balance deficits, and reduced reserve can increase cost.
Nonvascular transfemoralAbout +41% vs able-bodied walking.Loss of knee increases hip work and gait complexity.
Vascular transfemoralAbout +102% vs able-bodied walking.Very high energy demand; realistic mobility goals and cardiopulmonary reserve matter greatly.
Clinical interpretation
Energy cost increases further with poor socket fit, pain, contracture, hip weakness, poor balance, assistive-device dependence, slow walking speed, and cardiopulmonary disease.

10. Rehabilitation programme and exercise prescriptions

Progress from alignment and control to functional mobility.

Strengthening priorities

TargetWhy it mattersExamples
Hip extensorsUpright posture and resistance to hip flexion.Bridging, prone hip extension, sit-to-stand, resisted standing hip extension.
Hip abductorsPelvic stability and reduced lateral trunk lean.Side-lying hip abduction, band abduction, lateral step control.
Knee extensorsRise from chair and transtibial stance stability.Long-arc quads, sit-to-stand, wall-supported mini-squat when safe.
TrunkCenter-of-mass control and fewer compensatory strategies.Seated reaching, anti-rotation tasks, supported standing weight shifts.

Balance progression

ProgressionExamplesKey cue
Supported static standingParallel bars, symmetrical stance, mirror feedback.Load the prosthetic side gradually; keep pelvis level.
Weight shiftingSide-to-side, forward-backward, controlled reaching.Move center of mass without collapsing to the intact side.
Reduced hand supportTwo hands to one hand to fingertip support.Maintain upright trunk and steady breathing.
Dynamic balanceTurning, object transfer, step taps, obstacle negotiation.Prioritize quality and safety over speed.
Sensory challengeFoam, altered vision, dual task only after basic balance is safe.Progress cautiously in diabetic neuropathy.
Sit-to-stand
Purpose: functional hip and knee extensor strength, controlled weight transfer, postural control, and static-to-dynamic balance.

Starter dose: 5-10 repetitions × 2-3 sets, 3-5 days/week. Progress by reducing hand support, increasing repetitions, lowering chair height, adding a 3-5 second standing pause, then adding task complexity.
Water-bottle endurance exercise
Use a light bottle for low-load, high-repetition resistance to train local endurance of hip and trunk muscles. Begin around RPE 3-5/10, 15-20 repetitions × 2-3 sets, 2-3 days/week. Increase repetitions before load. Prioritize hip abduction and extension for pelvic stabilization during prosthetic stance and gait.

11. Assessment framework and outcome measures

Pair a measure with a clinical decision.

AMP / AMPnoPRO
Useful for pre-prosthetic and prosthetic mobility capacity, balance, transfers and gait potential.
5 Times Sit-to-Stand
Use time plus quality: hand support, asymmetrical loading, pain, loss of balance, and prosthetic tolerance. Do not use a single cut-off in isolation.
10-m Walk Test / 2- or 6-min Walk Test
Quantify walking speed and endurance; interpret in relation to safety, fatigue, device use, environment and participation goals.

Recall cards

Click “Reveal answer” after attempting each question.

Definition

What is a traumatic neuroma?

A disorganized regenerative nerve ending formed when a transected nerve regenerates without a suitable distal target. It may cause focal neuropathic pain and mechanical hypersensitivity.
Biomechanics

Why does knee flexion contracture reduce standing tolerance?

It keeps the ground-reaction force posterior to the knee, creating an external flexion moment. The quadriceps must work continuously to prevent collapse, increasing fatigue.
Prevention

What is the classic positioning error after transtibial amputation?

Putting a pillow under the knee or sitting for long periods with the knee flexed, which promotes knee-flexion contracture.
Prescription

Name the essential elements of a prosthetic prescription.

Socket, interface/liner, suspension, pylon/frame, foot and ankle, knee or hip unit if relevant, and accessories such as rotators or cosmetic cover.
Clinical reasoning

Why protect the contralateral foot in a diabetic transtibial amputee?

It bears greater load during asymmetrical gait and transfers, while neuropathy can mask pressure injury or ulcer development. Daily inspection and appropriate footwear are essential.
Rehabilitation

What is the quality target during sit-to-stand training?

Controlled symmetrical loading, upright standing, stable balance, and slow controlled descent, rather than simply completing repetitions quickly.
Pain

What does low-frequency TENS do for a painful neuroma?

It is an adjunct for short-term pain modulation that may improve socket wear, weight-bearing tolerance, and participation in rehabilitation. It does not eliminate the structural neuroma.
Gait

What is one key effect of hip flexion contracture on gait?

It prevents terminal hip extension and upright stacked alignment, promoting forward trunk/pelvic compensation and often shortening the contralateral step.
Systems

What makes gait energy cost higher than expected?

Pain, poor socket fit, contracture, hip weakness, poor balance, device dependence, slow speed, cardiopulmonary disease, and dysvascular comorbidity.
Gait analysis

What is the first reasoning step when you see an amputee gait deviation?

Identify the gait phase and visible deviation first. Then distinguish a prosthetic cause, such as fit, alignment, length, or component stiffness, from a patient factor such as pain, weakness, contracture, poor balance, or fear of loading.

Analytical quiz

Attempt the question, then reveal the clinical reasoning below.

1. A transtibial amputee stands with persistent knee flexion and reports rapid quadriceps fatigue. What is the most important biomechanical explanation?

Answer: The ground-reaction force tends to remain posterior to the knee, creating an external knee-flexion moment. The quadriceps must generate an internal extension moment to prevent buckling.

2. A diabetic transtibial amputee has a focal sharp electric stump pain, positive Tinel sign, and pain only when the socket presses over one point. What is the most likely primary pain generator?

Answer: Painful neuroma. The immediate clinical priorities are unloading the focal pressure, reviewing socket/interface and alignment, confirming clinically or with ultrasound when needed, and referring if persistent or function-limiting.

3. Your patient repeatedly pushes up mostly through the intact limb during sit-to-stand. What should be your main training target?

Answer: Symmetrical loading and controlled weight transfer. Use a firm chair, appropriate support, mirror feedback, pain monitoring, and graded progression. Protect the intact diabetic foot from chronic overload.

4. Why should foam and eyes-closed balance tasks be introduced cautiously in a diabetic amputee with peripheral neuropathy?

Answer: Peripheral neuropathy reduces reliable sensory feedback from the feet and limb. Altering surface or vision removes compensatory inputs, so progress only after basic supported balance and safe weight shifting are established.

5. A patient has hip flexion contracture after prolonged wheelchair use. Which standing compensation is most expected?

Answer: Forward trunk and pelvic compensation. The patient cannot bring the hip into full extension, so the body cannot align efficiently over the prosthetic base of support.

6. A patient wants a prosthesis “to walk normally.” Give a more complete rehabilitation target.

Model answer: “The target is to restore the highest feasible level of safe, energy-efficient, and independent mobility, while preventing contracture, skin injury, falls, contralateral-limb overload, and participation restriction. The immediate goals may include tolerable prosthetic weight bearing, stable standing, safe transfers, and progressive household or community ambulation.”

References and source notes

Use alongside local protocols and patient-specific clinical judgement.

  1. Department of Veterans Affairs and Department of Defense. Clinical Practice Guideline for Rehabilitation of Individuals with Lower Limb Amputation. 2025. VA/DoD guideline page.
  2. Webster JB, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Rehabilitation of Lower Limb Amputation: An Update from the Department of Veterans Affairs and Department of Defense. Am J Phys Med Rehabil. 2019;98(9):820-829. doi:10.1097/PHM.0000000000001213.
  3. American Physical Therapy Association. Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Rehabilitation of Individuals with Lower Limb Amputation. Updated resource page, 2024. APTA resource.
  4. Bus SA, et al. Guidelines on the prevention of foot ulcers in persons with diabetes: IWGDF 2023 update. Diabetes Metab Res Rev. 2024;40:e3651. IWGDF prevention guideline.
  5. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence. Targeted muscle reinnervation for managing limb amputation pain. HTG750, 2025. NICE HTG750.
  6. Peters BR, et al. Targeted muscle reinnervation for the management of pain in the setting of major limb amputation. SAGE Open Med. 2020;8:2050312120959180. doi:10.1177/2050312120959180.
  7. Original course material: uploaded OneNote file, Amputation-Prosthetics.one, reorganised into the accompanying study guide. Retained figures are included for personal educational use.
  8. Embedded figures from the uploaded study notes: Medicare Functional Classification Levels (K0-K4), major muscle activity during gait cycle, amputee gait abnormalities, and lower-extremity prosthetic component options. Captions have been corrected to match the source figures.
Educational note
This page is designed for study and clinical reasoning practice. Prosthetic component selection, pain intervention, wound care, and surgical referral should follow individual assessment, local pathways, and specialist input.
